column4: Question
Is there a clearly defined activity zone, and what defines it visually?
Can a child intuitively understand where to enter the activity, where to stand/sit, and where to leave?
Is the activity zone visibly separated from storage / other room functions?
What is the central "anchor object" of the activity, and is it clearly the focal point?
List the distinct materials available at the activity zone, with approximate counts.
Are materials within child-reach (at the height a 3–5 year old can access independently)?
Which senses are engaged by the activity (visual, tactile, auditory, olfactory, kinesthetic)?
What is the narrative or theme of the space that immerses the child?
Does the space feel calm and inviting, or chaotic and overstimulating?
Is there a "wow" / surprise element that would naturally pull a child's curiosity?
Are there visible safety concerns (hazards, sharp objects, unstable furniture, slippery floors, swallowable small parts)?
Is the space ready for the activity — set up before children arrive, with non-activity materials cleared, materials grouped meaningfully?
What is the learning objective of the activity, inferable from the setup alone (without the teacher explaining it)?
Is the activity teacher-directed, child-directed, or mixed?
What developmental skills is this activity targeting (fine motor, gross motor, language, social-emotional, cognitive, creative)?
Are choice or variation materials available so children can personalize their engagement (different colors / textures / tools)?
Is there room for individual creativity within the structure, or is there a single "right" outcome expected?
Is the activity's complexity suitable for a 3–5 year old?
Is the activity duration realistic for the attention span of this age group?
Can children at different skill levels engage meaningfully with the same setup?
Are there visible extensions (harder challenges) for children who finish quickly or want more?
Are there visible supports / scaffolds for children who need more help?
Is the activity equally accessible and inviting for all children, including girls, children with disabilities, and children from different cultural or linguistic backgrounds?
Can children start the activity independently without waiting for adult instruction?
Can children replenish, swap, or return materials on their own during the activity?
What is the teacher's overall tone — warm, neutral, or stern?
Does the teacher use children's names when addressing them?
Are there visible signs of shared positive moments (smiling, laughter, warm touches, enthusiasm)?
Are there moments of visible teacher frustration, irritation, or impatience?
Does the teacher use sarcasm, a harsh tone, or a raised/shouting voice at any point?
Are threats, punishment language, or warnings used?
Does the teacher make negative comments about children's behavior, abilities, or character?
Is any child publicly shamed, singled out negatively, or compared unfavorably to others?
Are there any instances where children may be unsafe — bad touch, physical roughness, abrupt handling, or unnecessary restriction of children?
Does the teacher notice when a child needs help, is upset, or is hesitating?
Does the teacher respond to individual children's emotional cues (distress, excitement, confusion)?
How does the teacher address problems when they arise?
Do children seek out the teacher for help, comfort, or to share something?
Does the teacher invite children's choices, ideas, or opinions during the activity?
Are children physically free to move, shift positions, or take breaks?
Does the teacher follow children's leads or interests at times, vs sticking strictly to the plan?
Are children's contributions (questions, comments, made-up rules) welcomed or shut down?
Are expectations and rules for the activity communicated clearly to children?
How does the teacher manage disruptions in the classroom?
How does the teacher handle a child who seems distressed?
How does the teacher handle conflicts between children?
How does the teacher handle a child who refuses to participate or seems disengaged?
Are transitions (start, mid-activity shifts, cleanup) smooth and quick, or messy and slow?
How many minutes are spent on the activity vs setup, transition, and waiting?
Are there any lulls or moments where the activity stalls and loses momentum?
Does the activity have a clear wrap-up / ending, or does it fade out?
Which instructional formats does the teacher use to engage children (e.g. songs, hands-on, demonstration, gesture, storytelling, movement, role-play)?
Are children given hands-on opportunities to do the activity, vs just watching or listening?
Does the teacher ask "why" or "how" questions that prompt children to think (e.g. "what do you think will happen if…")?
Does the teacher make connections to real-world experiences or to what children already know?
Does the teacher encourage children to be creative, generate their own ideas, or solve problems themselves?
Does the teacher offer hints, prompts, or partial answers rather than giving the full answer right away?
Does the teacher adjust the level of support based on individual children's needs?
Does the teacher follow up on children's responses with additional questions or extensions?
Is encouragement specific (praising effort, strategy, or specific actions) or generic ("good job")?
Are the teacher's questions mostly open-ended (extending thinking) or closed (yes/no, one-word answers)?
Does the teacher use rich, varied vocabulary appropriate to the activity?
Does the teacher name objects, actions, and feelings explicitly as they happen?
Does the teacher repeat and extend children's language (e.g. child says "red," teacher says "yes, a bright red roller")?
What number of children appear actively engaged at the peak of the activity vs disengaged?
What number of children were engaged for at least 80% of the time vs what number of children were NOT engaged for 80% of the time?
Do children try the activity in different ways, or stick to one rigid approach?
How many children asked a question during the activity?
What questions did the children ask?
Do children start the activity on their own, or wait for adult instruction?
Do children make their own choices during the activity (which color, which tool, what to make)?
Do children solve small problems themselves (e.g. paint not flowing, sharing a tool) before asking for help?
Are children visibly happy, relaxed, and at ease?
Do children show frustration, anxiety, or distress? How frequent and how intense?
Do children seem confident in their actions, or hesitant and seeking constant approval?
Do children interact with each other (talking, gesturing, sharing materials)?
Is there cooperation between children — working together, helping, or sharing space considerately?
Are there conflicts between children? How are they handled (by themselves, by teacher, or escalated)?
Are quieter or less-engaged children included by their peers, or left out?
Do children verbalise what they're doing, thinking, or noticing?
Do children try to extend or modify the activity (adding their own variations)?
Do children produce or create something concrete during the activity (artwork, structure, story)?
What are the minutes where the children speak vs where the teacher speaks?
Are ideas co-constructed (teacher and child build something together) or just transmitted (teacher tells, child receives)?
Are there shared moments of wonder, surprise, laughter, or discovery between teacher and children?
Does the teacher physically join children's play / activity, or direct from outside?
Does every child get individual attention from the teacher at some point in the activity?
Are there children who dominate the teacher's attention vs children who get very little?
Does the teacher actively bring quieter / less-participating children into the conversation?
Does each child speak at least once during the class?
Are children's unusual, off-script, or "wrong" answers welcomed and explored, or shut down?
